coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence enhancement of solid-state carbon dots by structure control for wide range laser threshold regulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Zefeng
Zhang, Shaofeng
Yao, Xin
Lin, Ye
Chen, Zhenhui
Zhang, Wenfei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have drawn widespread attention due to the characteristics of low cost, innocuity, environment friendliness, and distinctive optical performances. However, superior fluorescence is normally reported from CDs in liquid solution, which is not suitable for optoelectronic applications. Aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which inhibits the emission of CDs in solid-state, is always observed when they are transformed into solid state. Herein, a facile solvothermal route of in situ fabricating carbon dots for enhancing the photoluminescence of solid-state carbon dots by structure control is reported. Via the addition of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), the carbon dots are in situ formed in SiO2 network preventing ACQ with the structure of solid-state carbon dots morphing into amorphous nanoparticles from crystalline nanoparticles. Significant quantum yield (QY) enhancement than that of CDs without TEOS is observed (from 0.05 % to 14.02 %). By controlling light scattering in laser propagation, solid-state CDs are utilized as gain materials deriving blue emissive random lasers with wide threshold modulation ranging from 37.66 kW/cm2 to 405.74 kW/cm2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Random laser
Threshold modulation
Photoluminescence enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CA-CDs, CAT-CDs, Sp-CT and Sp-AT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterizations of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs. (a) TEM image of CA-CDs. Inset: particles size distribution diagrams and error bars (left) and HRTEM image (right) of CA-CDs. (b) TEM image of CAT-CDs. Inset: particles size distribution diagrams. (c) partially enlarged TEM image of CAT-CDs; inset: SEM image of CAT-CDs. HADDF (d) and element distribution maps (e-f) of CAT-CDs.
Structural characterizations of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs. (a-b) XRD (c) FTIR (d) XPS of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs, respectively.
High-resolution XPS spectra corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Si2p of CAT-CDs (a-d) and CA-CDs (e-h) respectively.

(a) General structures schematic diagrams and (b) schematics of in situ fabrication of CAT-CDs and CA-CDs. (c) PL emission and excitation spectra of CAT-CDs and CA-CDs. (d) PL decay curves of CAT-CDs and CAT-CDs. (e) PL emission and excitation spectra Sp-CT and Sp-AT. (f) PL intensity comparison of CAT-CDs, CA-CDs, Sp-CT, and Sp-AT. (g) PL decay curves of Sp-CT and Sp-AT.

Laser spectra of CAT-CDs with different CDs densities, corresponding to CAT-CDs to epoxy resin mass ratios of (a-d) 1:2, 1:44, 1:120, and 1:240. Laser threshold of different CDs densities corresponding to (e) 1:2, (f) 1:44, (h) 1:120, and (i) 1:240, respectively. (g) The dependence of lasing threshold on the density of CAT-CDs. Inset is the schematic configuration of random laser. D M means dielectric mirror and Al M means Al mirror.
Ratio of chemical bonds in CAT-CDs and CA-CDs.
Ratio of chemical bonds in CAT-CDs and CA-CDs.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence enhancement of solid-state carbon dots by structure control for wide range laser threshold regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have drawn widespread attention due to the characteristics of low cost, innocuity, environment friendliness, and distinctive optical performances. However, superior fluorescence is normally reported from CDs in liquid solution, which is not suitable for optoelectronic applications. Aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which inhibits the emission of CDs in solid-state, is always observed when they are transformed into solid state. Herein, a facile solvothermal route of in situ fabricating carbon dots for enhancing the photoluminescence of solid-state carbon dots by structure control is reported. Via the addition of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), the carbon dots are in situ formed in SiO2 network preventing ACQ with the structure of solid-state carbon dots morphing into amorphous nanoparticles from crystalline nanoparticles. Significant quantum yield (QY) enhancement than that of CDs without TEOS is observed (from 0.05 % to 14.02 %). By controlling light scattering in laser propagation, solid-state CDs are utilized as gain materials deriving blue emissive random lasers with wide threshold modulation ranging from 37.66 kW/cm2 to 405.74 kW/cm2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industry and technology, miniature solid-state lasers play an increasingly important role in the fields of communication, illumination, biomedicine, and biosensors due to their advantages of easy integration and spectral tunability [1–5]. Colloidal quantum dots, perovskite, and organic dyes have been served as good candidates for the gain materials of solid-state lasers exhibiting intrinsic toxicity, instability, and oncogenicity [6–12]. Therefore, carbon dot, a new type of carbon-based innocuous, economical and stable nanoparticles, is an ambitious alternate serving as a gain material for miniature solid-state lasers. Moreover, the luminescence of carbon dots is probably weakened or even quenched by the aggregation in solid-state or highly concentrated solution, which limits the application of carbon dots [13,14]. To restrain aggregation-caused quenching, researchers have proposed various strategies such as surface functional groups modification or matrix-assisted methods.

In the last few years, researchers devoted themselves exploiting carbon dots laser gain medium. Inspired by the first report employing carbon dots as active materials of Zhang in 2012 [15], the number of articles reported by researchers using this green, non-toxic, and biocompatible material for lasing has constantly increased. Qu et al. achieved green lasing emission from carbon dots in ethanol solution in a liner long Fabry-Perot cavity in 2013 [16]. Zhang et al. synthesized surface functionalized CDs exhibiting a high quantum yield of 68 % under 450 nm excitation [17]. The research group of Liu reported a planar waveguide structure formed by CDs with amplified spontaneous emission, which provided effective guidelines for low-threshold solid-state carbon dots lasers in 2016 [18]. From 2023 to 2024, Lei and Li et al. employed biological pollutant cyanobacteria as a carbon source to synthesize several solid-state carbon dots with high quantum yield (exceeding 40 %) for white LED and visual ratiometric fluorescence sensors [19,20]. In addition, various types of carbon dots lasers with different applications are reported such as ultra stable and highly efficient blue, green, and red random lasers with low-thresholds for lighting [21], lasers with a gain medium composed of carbon dots for tunable narrowband lasing beam output [22] and random lasers producing vortex with flexible control of the topological charge and modes [23]. However, these researches concerned more about lasing wavelengths of CDs rather than laser threshold regulation [2,15,16,22,24–31]. There are very few articles reported laser threshold regulation [32]. Zhang etc. reported regulation of thresholds of CDs laser from 341.6 to 165.5 mJ/cm2 in solution via rejigging the molar ratio of precursors [31]. However, the threshold range of regulation was relatively narrow. Therefore, a strategy for achieving solid-state CDs laser with wide range threshold modulation is still lacking.

In this paper, blue-emissive solid-state carbon dots with TEOS (CAT-CDs) were in situ fabricated in silica matrix from citric acid (CA), anilinomethyl triethoxysilane (AMTOS), and TEOS with the advantages of resist-aggregation and readily devising random laser cavities. Causing of the addition of TEOS, the structure of carbon dots was transformed from crystallin nanoparticles to amorphous nanoparticles, and, meanwhile, carbon dots were embedded in SiO2 networks resulting in resist-aggregation by in situ fabrication. The fluorescence QY of CDs embedded in SiO2 networks has been enhanced by 14.02 %. Moreover, the fabricated CDs were utilized to realize solid-state carbon dots laser with tunable laser threshold regulation for a wide range from 37.66 kW/cm2 to 405.74 kW/cm2 by modifying the density of solid-state CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CA-CDs, CAT-CDs, Sp-CT and Sp-AT
ce:section.ce:para: 0.83 mmol CA and 0.93 mmol AMTOS were dissolved in mixture of 5 mL H2O and 5 mL of EtOH by ultrasonication. Then the solution was transferred into 50 mL teflonlined stainless-steel autoclave, heated to 150℃, and kept at this temperature for 3 h. Then the resulting precipitates were washed using the mixture of ethanol and water (volume ratio of 2:1) by three times to remove unreacted precursors. Finally, CA-CDs were obtained as light-yellow powder after drying. For CAT-CDs, 0.83 mmol CA, 0.93 mmol AMTOS and 12.5 mmol TEOS were dissolved in the solution mixed by 5 mL H2O and 5 mL of EtOH. Other processes are the same as synthesis of CA-CDs. The fabrication of Sp-CT (Sp-AT) is similar to that of CA-CDs with the different precursors. The raw materials of Sp-CT (Sp-AT) are 0.83 mmol CA and 12.5 mmol TEOS (0.93 mmol AMTOS and 12.5 mmol TEOS).

CAT-CDs were mixed with epoxy resin with mass ratios of 1:2, 1:11, 1:44, 1:120 and 1:240 by continuing stirring to form CAT-CDs/ epoxy composites. Simultaneously, 15 μL mixture was drop-casted on an Al mirror to form CAT-CDs/ epoxy composite gain layer. After that, a dielectric mirror was covered on the gain layer. Finally, the laser cavity was formed by heating at 60 ℃ for 1 h.

A FEI Tecnai G2 F30 high resolution transmission eletrcon microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI, America) is used to investigate the morphology of the CA-CDs and CAT-CDs. A JSM-6490LA scanning electrcon microscope (SEM, JEOL, Japan) is used to investigate the SEM images of CAT-CDs. The fluorescence spectra and decay curves of CA-CDs, CAT-CDs, Sp-CT and Sp-AT are measured on an FS5 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Neodymium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet (Nd:YAG) pulsed laser (355 nm, 6 ns, 10 Hz, Continuum Surelite, California, USA) with an optical parameter oscillator (Continuum Horizon, California, USA) and a 355 nm frequency-tripled Continuum Powerlite DLS 9010Q-switched Nd:YAG pulsed (∼6 ns, 10 Hz) laser act as the excitation source for lasing measurements. Laser spectra are characterized by a HARIOBA iHR320 spectrophotometer (Minami-ku, Kyoto, Japan) and an Oriel MS257 monochromator via an optical fiber. The reported optical measurements are conducted on solid state CDs (power).

The morphologies of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs are shown in Fig. 1a and b. It is observed in Fig. 1a that quasi-spherical particles with an average diameter of 2.1 nm (as shown in the histogram in the upper-left inset of Fig. 1a) are dispersed homogeneously. Lattice spacing of the particles is measured to be 0.21 nm, which is attributed to (100) facet of graphite, as observed in the HRTEM image in the upper-right inset of Fig. 1a [33,34]. It is also observed in Fig. 1b that amorphous CDs with an average size of 4.27 nm are obtained. Most of the CAT-CDs were amorphous and devoid of detectable lattice fringes, indicating the structure of CAT-CD should be a crosslinked network instead of a graphitized core. This reveals that structures of CA-CDs are altered by embedding amorphous carbon dots in SiO2 matrix. The SiO2 particles with CAT-CDs embedded in the matrix are irregular microscale particles caused by grinding (as shown in the SEM image in the inset of Fig. 1c). Element distributions of CAT-CDs (Fig. 1d–i) show that C, N, O, and Si, which are all homogeneously distributed in CAT-CDs, are predominant elements in CAT-CDs with N being present in lesser quantities. This indicates that carbon dots are homogeneously embedded in SiO2 network. The TEM and HRTEM images of Sp-AT presented in Fig. S1 (a) illustrate that these particles are evenly distributed CDs with a size distribution between 1 and 4 nm (Fig. S2). The HRTEM image demonstrates a well-resolved lattice fringe in Sp-AT with a lattice distance of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene (inset of Fig. S1 (a)). As shown in Fig. S1 (b), no identifiable structures or lattice fringes were observed in Sp-CT. The Sp-CT may be a type of fluorescent molecules. Furthermore, HADDF-STEM pictures (Fig. 1d-i) are utilized to demonstrate the existence of carbon dots structures. The distributions of C, N, O, and Si atoms are proportional to the bright regions in HADDF images, indicating that they do not originate from background, but from CAT-CDs, since the background does not contain N and Si atoms. What’s more, the presence of N atoms in Fig. 1g substantiates the existence of CDs in bright regions of HADDF.

In the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern ( Fig. 2a–b), both CA-CDs and CAT-CDs display a broad peak at 22°, which is estimated to originate from the highly disordered carbon atoms and amorphous silica. Functional groups at the surfaces of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs were analyzed through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements (Fig. 2c). In general, the absorption bands at 3200–3530 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of -OH and -NH2. In CA-CDs, the intense absorption peaks at 2981 cm−1, 1708 cm−1, and 1635 cm−1 correspond to the presence of C-H, CO, and -CONH, respectively, which reveals that carbon cores are formed via condensation reactions between carboxyl groups of CA and aniline groups of AMTOS [35–37]. The absorption peaks located at 1000–1200 cm−1, and 798 cm−1 correspond to the signals of Si-O-Si, and Si-O-H [38], respectively, indicating the formation of silica on CA-CDs surfaces due to hydrolysis and condensation of siloxane groups within AMTOS. However, in CAT-CDs, the stretching vibration intensity of -CONH is declining, which means that the hydrolysis reaction of TEOS and CA is in competition with the hydrolysis and condensation reaction of CA and AMTOS. As shown in Fig. S3, the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) of CAT-CDs reveals broad diffused halos, which is a characteristic of amorphous materials. Notably, the absence of diffraction-enhanced features indicates that the sample lacks crystallinity with low degree of graphitization [39]. What’s more, XRD spectrum displayed a prominent broad diffraction peak centered at an angle of 22° (Fig. 2b). This observation indicates the inherently amorphous nature of the carbon dots [40]. The partially enlarged TEM image of CAT-CDs (Fig. 1(c)) shows the absence of lattice fringes. By aforementioned characterization methods, it is confirmed that CAT-CDs are amorphous. The amorphous structure of the CAT-CDs may be attributed to the heterogeneous growth of carbon dots in silica and AMTOS frameworks [41].

Moreover, a large amount of TEOS also undergo hydrolysis reaction with H2O to form a Si-O-Si network on CAT-CDs surfaces. The SiO2 network encapsulates the CAT-CDs with a linkage of Si-O-Si bonds. Particularly, the stretching vibration intensity of N-H bonds at 1557 cm−1 decreases from CA-CDs to CAT-CDs with emission enhancement, which is also due to the competition between TEOS and AMTOS. These combined characterization results demonstrate that CAT-CDs are amorphous nanoparticles with abundant functional groups (especially amide and amino groups) on their surfaces. The FTIR spectra of Sp-AT and Sp-CT are shown in Fig. S4 (a). The XPS and HRXPS of Sp-AT and Sp-CT are also displayed in Fig. S4 (b), Fig. S5, and Fig. S6, respectively. What’s more, the analyses of the structures of Sp-AT and Sp-CT are presented in Supporting Information. In Fig. 2d, the full survey of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) scan displays four typical peaks: C 1 s (285 eV), N 1 s (400 eV), O 1 s (532 eV), and Si 2p (103 eV), which indicates that the two composites consist of the same elements.

The C 1 s band in the high-resolution scans of both CA-CDs and CAT-CDs ( Fig. 3a, e) can be deconvoluted into five peaks at 283.95, 284.69, 285.95, 286.98, and 288.88 eV, corresponding to C-Si, C-C/CC, C-N/C-O, CO, OC-O, respectively. The primary distinction lies in the increased proportion of the O-CO groups in CAT-CDs compared to CA-CDs, alongside a decrease in the C-Si groups within CAT-CDs. The N 1 s band contains three peaks at 398.81 eV, 399.75 eV, and 400.76 eV, representing N-Si, C-N-C, and N-H, respectively (Fig. 3b, 3f). The O 1 s band can be divided into three peaks at 531.65 eV, 532.28 eV, and 532.99 eV, representing CO/OC-N, OC-O, and Si-O/C-O, individually (Fig. 3c, 3g). The increase of Si-O groups in CAT-CDs indicates the incorporation of a considerable amount of silica structure when compared to CA-CDs. Furthermore, the alterations in O-CO groups are consistent with the observations presented in the preceding text. The Si 2p band can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 102.96 eV, 103.41 eV for Si-OH, and Si-O-Si, respectively (Fig. 3d, 3h). In comparison to CA-CDs, the Si-C/Si-N groups in CAT-CDs are absent. At the same time, extensive Si-O-Si structures are fabricated. For CA-CDs, there is another band located at 101.44 eV, representing Si-C/Si-N (Fig. 3h)[36]. Statistical analysis indicates that percentages of OC-O and Si-O-Si groups have an obvious rise and CO/CO-N groups decrease, which implies that the reactions of TEOS with CA and H2O will change the structure of carbon cores and the distribution of surface groups, respectively (
Tables 1,2). Furthermore, as shown in Tables 1–2, the increase of O-CO in CAT-CDs indicates that the n–π* transition of the CO is the majority, which resulted in trapping of the excited-state energy by the surface states[42]. At the same time, as evidenced by FTIR and XPS data, the notable increase in Si-O-Si functional groups suggests that the formation of the silica network disperses CDs to prevent aggregation-caused quenching. Fig. S7 shows the absorption spectra of CAT-CDs, CA-CDs, Sp-AT, and Sp-CT, respectively. The four samples exhibit a distinct absorption peak at 246 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bond[19,20]. The absorption spectrum of Sp-AT displays a peak at 288 nm, which also corresponds to the π-π* transition of CC bond. What’s more, the absorption peak at 344 nm in CAT-CDs and a broad absorption peak at 384 nm in CA-CDs are assigned to the n-π* transition of CO group[36,37]. According to these characterizations the fluorescence of CAT-CDs originates from the π-π* transition of CC bonds in the carbon core and the n-π* transition of CO bonds on the surface. The silica network disperses the carbon dots to prevent aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), allowing CAT-CDs to exhibit intense emission with the highest quantum yield (14.02 %). Notably, CA-CDs do possess CC and CO, but, as a result of ACQ, CA-CDs exhibit weak emission. Besides, the fluorescence of Sp-CT and Sp-AT originates from the π-π* transition of CC. However, due to ACQ of Sp-AT, its quantum efficiency (0.39 %) is lower than that of CAT-CDs. Therefore, CAT-CDs demonstrate superior fluorescence emission and a longer fluorescence lifetime in comparison to that of CA-CDs and Sp-AT caused by severe aggregation of CDs ( Fig. 4c, d, g). Due to the severe aggregation of carbon dots, CA-CDs and Sp-AT exhibit weak emission and shorter fluorescence lifetimes.

The yellowish and yellow powder of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs was synthesized by solvothermal reaction of CA, AMTOS, and TEOS at 150 ℃ for 3 h in a mixture of ethanol and H2O (Fig. 4a, b). According to the mentioned morphological and structural characterization, the potential structures of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs are shown in Fig. 4a. The construction of CA-CDs is different from that of CAT-CDs, whose structure is altered by embedding CDs in a huge Si-O-Si network, causing obviously discrepant photoluminescence properties. The fluorescence spectra (PL) and excitation spectra of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs are displayed in Fig. 4c. The optimal excitation peak wavelength of CAT-CDs is located at 378 nm. Excited by optimal excitation wavelength, the CAT-CDs exhibit strong blue emission, which is stronger than that of CA-CDs. Furthermore, CAT-CDs are excitation dependent, which is a widely reported characteristic of CDs. The PL spectra of CAT-CDs are shown in Fig. S8. The spectra indicate a slight redshift as the excitation wavelength increases from 330 nm to 400 nm. When the excitation wavelengths range from 280 nm to 310 nm, the emission peaks of the spectra are observed at 496 nm. From 400 nm to 420 nm, the emission peaks of spectra are around 510 nm. The transitions of emission peaks are caused by different luminescence centers. Herein, according to the excitation spectra (Fig. 4c and Fig. S8), ultraviolet light (365 nm) is employed as the excitation source for random laser cavities. In addition, Luminescence Decay Curves of CA-CDs and CAT-CDs show their measured lifetimes to be 1.17 ns and 7.26 ns, respectively (Fig. 4d). This primarily results from the enhancement of stability of the excited state by encapsulation of CDs in the SiO2 matrix. Stemming from the above research, we propose a hypothesis that the addition of TEOS sustains the energy levels stability of carbon dots to reduce the nonradiative transition of CAT-CDs resulting in higher photoluminescence intensity than that of CA-CDs. To confirm this hypothesis, Sp-CT and Sp-AT were synthesized by using CA, TEOS and AMTOS, TEOS as raw materials, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4e, the optimal excitation wavelengths of Sp-CT and Sp-AT are both located around 380 nm with optimal emission wavelengths of 470 nm and 456 nm, respectively. What’s more, QY is defined as the ratio of the number of emitted photons to the number of absorbed photons. The QY of 14.02 % for CAT-CDs is the highest, followed by 9.61 % for Sp-CT, 0.39 % for Sp-AT, and 0.05 % for CA-CDs (Fig. S9, S10). This reveals that the fluorescence of four samples derives from distinct emitting centers. The detailed structures of four samples are shown in Fig. S11 to elucidate the variations in fluorescence emission among the samples. For CAT-CDs, the fluorescence mainly originates from the π-π* transition of CC group and n-π* transition of CO group. For CA-CDs, the sample exhibt minimal fluorescence as a result of ACQ. The fluorescence of Sp-AT is attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC in the aromatic ring [43]. AMTOS functions as a luminescent component within Sp-AT and serves as a “bridge” in CAT-CDs, facilitating the connection of CDs and silica networks to prevent ACQ. For Sp-CT, the fluorescence mainly originates from the π-π* transition of CC group this sample [44]. Lifetimes of Sp-CT and Sp-AT are measured to be 6.16 ns and 5.92 ns, respectively (Fig. 4g). The three samples with TEOS (CAT-CDs, Sp-CT, Sp-AT) exhibit higher QY and longer lifetimes than that of CA-CDs without TEOS indicating that TEOS does enhance fluorescence emission by modifying the structure of carbon dots to reduce nonradiative transition.

Given the increasing interest in the field of CDs, CDs-based lasers are nowadays garnering significant attention. Therefore, a stratagem which regulates laser threshold in a large power range is significance for promoting development of CDs-based lasers. Our proposed CAT-CDs can be used as a gain medium to realize CDs laser. More importantly, the laser threshold is regulated over a wide range by regulation of particle density of CAT-CDs. The schematic configuration of the random laser is shown in the inset of Fig. 5g. CAT-CDs solid state laser is constructed by sandwiching the CAT-CDs/epoxy composite between a dielectric mirror (D M) and an Al mirror (Al M) to form a laser cavity. It was observed that the CDs/epoxy film was about 140 μm thick (Fig. S12). The use of the dielectric mirror is to improve the longitudinal confinement of light inside the laser cavity. In this system, the mico-SiO2 efficiently increases optical scattering as a type of scattering media. The cavity and the mico-SiO2 play the role of optical resonator to extend the feedback path. Both the mico-SiO2 and the cavity jointly enhance the optical gain to promote the stimulated emission.

The lasing spectra and the light-light curves of the CAT-CDs solid state lasers with different CAT-CDs and epoxy mass ratios are shown in Fig. 5. Small bumps are formed in the spectra when the excitation power is below the threshold values. A few sharp peaks are observed (see the light blue curves of Fig. 5a-d) when the excitation power exceeds the threshold values (knots in the light-light curves shown in Fig. 5e, f, h, and i). With further increasing the excitation power, more lasing modes emerge from the spectra, covering the entire gain bandwidth. However, the distribution of the lasing modes is not uniform over the emission spectra. This is due to the random scattering of the light introduced by the unevenly distributed micro-SiO2. The inset of Fig. 5e reveals a threshold of 37.66 kW/cm2, when the mass ratio of CAT-CDs/epoxy is fixed at 1:2. The concentration of CAT-CDs in this random laser cavity is the highest, with a quality factor (Q factor) of 924 and the lowest laser threshold (Fig. 5e). For the laser cavity with mass ratio of CAT-CDs/epoxy fixed at 1:44 (Fig. 5b), the threshold is observed at 88.23 kW/cm2 (Fig. 5f) and a Q factor is 959. In Fig. 5c, the analysis of the laser spectra from a laser cavity with a 1:120 ratio of CAT-CDs/epoxy reveals the threshold of 208.59 kW/cm2 (Fig. 5h) and a Q factor of 835. For laser cavities of mass ratios of 1:240 (Fig. 5d), the threshold is 405.74 kW/cm2. The Q factors is 887. With increasing the proportion of CAT-CDs, thresholds of random solid-state lasers increase gradually. A specific random solid-state laser with a tunable threshold was prepared by adjusting the density of CAT-CDs in epoxy. The threshold modulation range can be adjusted by an order of magnitude from 37.66 kW/cm2 to 405.74 kW/cm2 (Fig. 5g). However, the adjustments of thresholds are not infinite but feasible within a constrained range. On the one hand, as the concentration of CDs decreases, the threshold will gradually increase while the fluorescence signal of the cavity will also gradually decrease. When the concentration is lower than a certain value, the signal-to-noise ratio of the spectrum becomes poor, making it challenging to differentiate the fluorescence signal. Additionally, the increase in threshold is not significant. At this point, reducing the concentration of CDs is meaningless. On the other hand, as the concentration of gain medium, specifically the concentration of CAT-CDs, diminishes, the optical cavity is unable to provide adequate gain to facilitate the generation of random lasers. Consequently, this modulation range was meticulously evaluated to prevent the occurrence of these scenarios.

In conclusion, blue-emitting solid-state carbon dots, whose QY is significantly higher than that of carbon dots without TEOS, embedded in SiO2 network to inhibit ACQ are in situ fabricated by CA, AMTOS and TEOS in mixture of H2O and ethanol. The TEM images were used to analyze the structural modifications in solid-state carbon dots with TEOS caused by the introduction of TEOS. The average size of solid-state carbon dots with TEOS increases from 2.07 nm to 4.72 nm with the morphology of solid-state carbon dots with TEOS morphing into amorphous nanoparticles from crystallin quasi-spherical nanoparticles of carbon dots without TEOS. Furthermore, considering the unique construction which is perceived as random laser cavity cooperating with sandwich structure to act as random laser cavity, solid-state carbon dots with TEOS were employed to fabricated random laser cavities with controllable thresholds by adjusting the density of CAT-CDs/epoxy from 37.66 kW/cm2 to 405.74 kW/cm2.

Yao Xin: Formal analysis. Lin Ye: Resources. Wu Zefeng: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhang Shaofeng: Validation, Formal analysis. Zhang Wenfei: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chen Zhenhui: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Science and Technology Projects of Shenzhen (20231121100725001), Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Photonics and Biophotonics (ZDSYS20210623092006020), National Natural Science Foundation of China (61875127).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2025.179207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lasing of carbon dots: chemical design, mechanisms, and bright future

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, S. Lu, Lasing of carbon dots: Chemical design, mechanisms, and bright future, Chem, 10 (2024) 134-171.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid-state red laser with a single longitudinal mode from carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, H. Song, L. Wang, J. Yu, B. Wang, Y. Hu, S.Q. Zang, B. Yang, S. Lu, Solid-state red laser with a single longitudinal mode from carbon dots, Angew. Chem.-Int. Edit., 60 (2021) 25514-25521.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The light of carbon dots: from mechanism to applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, S. Lu, The light of carbon dots: From mechanism to applications, Matter, 5 (2022) 110-149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White light-emitting devices based on carbon dots' electroluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Wang, Y.H. Chen, C.Y. Liu, D.G. Ma, White light-emitting devices based on carbon dots' electroluminescence, Chem. Commun., 47 (2011) 3502-3504.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Chung, J. Kim, C.B. Park, Photonic carbon dots as an emerging nanoagent for biomedical and healthcare applications, ACS Nano, 14 (2020) 6470-6497.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Qin, A.S.D. Sandanayaka, C. Zhao, T. Matsushima, D. Zhang, T. Fujihara, C. Adachi, Stable room-temperature continuous-wave lasing in quasi-2D perovskite films, Nature, 585 (2020) 53-57.
Y.-S. Park, J. Roh, B.T. Diroll, R.D. Schaller, V.I. Klimov, Colloidal quantum dot lasers, Nat. Rev. Mater., 6 (2021) 382-401.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, Realization of lasing emission from the different perovskite quantum dot–doped materials, Plasmonics, 18 (2023) 607-615.

Das, Bovine serum abumin amplified reactive oxygen species generation from anthrarufin-derived carbon dot and concomitant nanoassembly for combination antibiotic-photodynamic therapy application, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces, 11 (2019) 33273-33284.
